source: Gaze Glass — A Family of Mortals (2).docx
heading: 2 Sahil
paragraphs: Marok floated high in the ocean-blue sky, cool wind blowing against his fur, and watched Sahil Shah dash between tall, luscious Mango trees and up the mountain. The mortal jumped effortlessly over orange and red fruit without a care in the world. He had just arrived in the cool mountains of Rishikesh from the blistering jungles of Kerala.  Despite being ten, Sahil could outrun most adults; he could speak as quickly, too. As he ran through a dense rainforest, wide, brown leaves lifted from the earth in his wake.

Sahil chuckled and ate the minty wind. Then pulled the damp collar of his maroon shirt up to his forehead, pressing the cotton firmly against his skin. He looked up at the sky through the garment and smiled at the sliver of sunshine that touched his face. “I can see you!” he shouted to the sun peeking from behind through gray clouds, his honey eyes sparkling with joy.

He stopped to greet two cows that reeked of manure—one jet black and the other snow white. He followed them as they bobbed their heads in search of grass, their black and white coats standing out vividly against the brown and gold of the jungle. Sahil stumbled to a halt. He leaned over, and peered into a puddle. The boy’s oily brown face was slick with sweat and his jaw was sharp enough to cut stone. Sahil’s supple black hair was shaved at the back and sides, with the top left longer.

Sahil lifted his head and straightened his spine. He patted the cows’ butts and smiled. With a long, deep breath, he took off running, reaching full speed again within minutes. His grin widened when he spotted a moss green cottage with a rusty roof. Around it, talkative white peacocks shimmered and clucked. Their lustrous feathers swept golden leaves from the earth.

Sahil gasped. And the peacocks all turned at once to face him. They raised and protracted their feathers toward the groggy sky, revealing shiny silver eyes. Then a smiley brown boy with long dark hair hopped between the birds. Sahil panicked and bolted forward, his tongue locked firmly between his lips. Twigs snapped under Sahil’s feet. The boy between the birds heard the sound and turned fast. A peacock cried, casting panic all around. Then the birds scattered and screamed. Sahil flinched and fell on his back.

Sahil’s eyes rolled up; he followed the pearly buttons on the boy's sleeveless white linen shirt and saw cocoa-colored eyes and a bright white smile. The thin inky wisps framing his face were curly and touched his shoulders like the bottom of a curtain. Sahil stared at a thin chain made of gold dangling from his right ear, a cracked jewel at the end.
“Mera naam hai—” the boy with the peacocks started, meaning: ”My name is—” in Hindi.
Sahil opened his mouth like a crocodile and cried. “I don’t—I don’t speak—Hindi!”
The boy with the peacocks blinked and placed his right hand atop his chest. “Arjun.”
“Oh!” Sahil said and placed his left hand on his own racing heart. “Sah—hill.”
Arjun pointed to Sahil and exclaimed with glee, “Sahil!”
Sahil pointed back at Arjun, his face brightening. “Arjun!”
After an eternal pause, the boys laughed. They wandered through the jungle with the gossiping peacocks. Sahil pressed his dry tongue against the roof of his hot mouth and swallowed.
“Tum theek ho?” Arjun asked, meaning: “Are you okay?”
Marok blinked and scratched his left ear with a back leg and cackled. “ It’s time!”

A chill wind crept around the cottage and grabbed the smiley boys. Then the fox jumped into a pile of brown leaves and the boys turned to face the sound. Marok rolled under a sheet of leaves and cackled. Dark green flames spread out from underneath his body in every direction, eating the foliage. A wave of lime-green embers raced up the swollen trunk of a petrified mango tree and stretched across wiry branches. The peacocks cried as they scattered, leaving Arjun and Sahil behind.
Suddenly, a burning branch fell between the boys, separating them with a wall of green flame.

“Arjun!” Sahil shouted, backing away as heat stung his face, but he could not find the boy with the peacocks. “Arjun!” he tried again. When Marok emerged from the fiery wall, Sahil took two steps backward and tripped. His calm cracked like the foliage under his feet. He fell on his back and smacked his head on a jagged rock. Seconds after, he got up and ran, but he didn’t get very far—he turned a corner and rushed into a peacock. Then, dead leaves shot up from the ground. White feathers swept his face and sharp talons cut his forehead. Sahil turned his face away from the bird and felt lines against his left cheek, leaves and twigs pressing against the back of his forehead. Foliage cracked from behind. The boy grasped the net. “Let me out!” he shouted.

The peacock twitched and screeched. Sahil tried to use his fingers to rake the wall, but he could barely move his arms. The peacock’s heavy body was contorted, pushing against the crown of his head. Sahil lowered his chin to his chest and peered through the net. The earth spun clockwise. Then he felt the net turn in the other direction. Back and forth, and over and over again until the peacock became still.
The snap of a twig startled Sahil and the bird. Between the cries and failed wing flaps, he saw two black sneakers. “Look what we have here,” said a deep, menacing voice.
The earth spun faster and faster as raindrops fell on his scalp. Deafening thunder rolled through Sahil’s bones. And the peacocks twisted and screamed.  “Let me out!” Sahil commanded.

The net immediately stopped spinning, and the black sneakers moved closer. Sahil saw bare knees before he noticed pale green eyes. The man had leathery brown skin, a thick, graying beard, and a long scar along his left cheek. The poacher’s face twisted into an eerie smile.
“A bonus,” he said, the corners of his lips curled up, revealing yellow teeth.
“Let me out!” Sahil growled. “Or I’ll kick your ass!”

Sahil held his breath and pulled at the net. When his exhale finally came, the poacher’s gaze shifted to Sahil’s clenched knuckles. Tree branches rustled above. The poacher looked up and smiled at the peacock. “I know someone who would pay a hefty price for you!” the poacher said, turning away. “The boy can be put to work.” He instructed his crew to lower the net into the back of a rusty red truck.

Moments before the car’s engine roared, Marok jumped into the back of the truck. The fox’s green eyes turned magenta and cast a glow on Sahil’s oily face. Then the fox exhaled magenta smoke from his snout. The wisps combined into The God of Beauty. She smiled at Sahil and placed her cold gemstone palm on the warm, bloody skin seeping through the net. Pink flames dissolved the fibers and the peacock crumbled into pink ash. Sahil landed in the ashes. He grabbed his face and gaped.
“Do not be afraid, Sahil Shah,” the Goddess said with a sugary voice.
Sahil blinked. “Who—who are you?”
“I have many names.”
“Are you an angel?”
“I helped make them.”
“You’re god?” Sahil asked, eyes twinkling.
“One of them,” the God of Beauty whispered and winked.
“No way!”
“Yes way!” she said in a singsong voice.

The God of Beauty clicked her rose quartz tongue, freezing time: the poachers’ scowls remained still, gray crows waited midair with their wings outstretched, and the truck lost its voice. Sahil stood up and twirled. The warm jungle was as still as death and quiet as a photograph.
“Little Sahil. We are not pleased with your world,” the Goddess said.
“Yeah. This place sucks.”
The God of Beauty laughed. “You are funny. But tell me … what would you do if you were its ruler?” she asked. “What would your world look like if you were a god?”
Sahil’s black brows lowered and his lips moved away from his teeth. “Make food free.”
“Serpents can eat for free,” the God of Beauty said, her smile growing, eyes twinkling.
“Yeah. They don’t pay for their food!” Sahil cried.
“Precisely. What else?”
“Priya and I go to Karate twice a week and Kendo once a week. I want to go to both every day!”
“And for what reason do you wish to fight?”
Sahil’s tongue poked out from between his lips. “I want nine more stripes on my black belt and I want to be the greatest samurai!” the God of Beauty smirked. “Do you want to become a god?”
Sahil frowned. “I don’t know if my mom will let me.”
“What if she said yes?”
The corners of Sahil’s lips lifted and he rubbed his palms together. “What do I have to do?”
“Say ‘I pledge my loyalty—body, mind, and spirit—to The God of Beauty’.”
Sahil put his hand over his heart and smiled. “I pledge my loyalty—body, mind, and spirit to the God of Beauty.”
“I will become a pledge for the judgment,” The God of Beauty continued.
“What’s the judgment?” Sahil asked, one brow rising.
“It’s a game we play. If you win, you become a god. Do you want to know the details?”
Sahil paused for two seconds. “Nah.” he said. “I will become a pledge for the judgment.”

The God of Beauty laughed, pulling Sahil into a hug with all ten hands, and clicked her tongue. A silver diamond-like lotus appeared between his eyes. Two petals bloomed from the center and fell on either side. The boy’s black pupils brightened to a beautiful shade of magenta and glowed. When Sahil blinked, the goddess had vanished and time came alive. He saw poachers walk to their rusty trucks. The sight of them made his skin turn to pink crystal. One of the poachers saw the glimmering boy and cocked his head to the side. “Oi! You see that, Zatik?” The poacher’s leader turned around.
Sahil looked into pale green eyes and screamed, his captor’s leathery skin turned to black tourmaline. Then the statue crumbled into dark sand.
The God of Beauty’s voice entered his mind and grew louder with each word.
Come. Let us fetch your family.
